---
title: Building the Capture-to-Publish Pipeline
date: 2026-06-21
excerpt: How this site turns a YouTube link or an article into a verified, published note and an evergreen topic, with the verbatim source kept private as ground truth. The architecture, the skills that gate it, and the bugs that bit.
tags: [build, pipeline, astro, agents]
---

import Mermaid from '../../../components/Mermaid.astro';

This is the machine behind the rest of the site. Share a link, and it comes out the other end as a
dated [Note](/working-intel/notes/) and a fold into an evergreen [Topic](/working-intel/topics/), with
the raw source archived privately so every claim can be checked against ground truth. This is the build
log for that pipeline: what it does, how it is wired, and where it broke.

**Applies:** [The Knowledge Flywheel](/working-intel/topics/knowledge-flywheel/) — this is that flywheel
made concrete.

## The flow

Three published artifacts, one private. The source archive is the ground truth the verifier checks
against; it never ships.

<Mermaid
	title="Capture → publish, with the gates that guard it"
	chart={`
flowchart TD
    L["link / inbox drop"] --> X["extract<br/>(transcript · webfetch · file)"]
    X --> A["archive verbatim<br/>(gitignored, private)"]
    A --> N["Note<br/>dated, + outside perspectives"]
    N --> T["Topic<br/>evergreen fold"]
    T --> B["Build<br/>apply it (you are here)"]

    A -. ground truth .-> V{{"claim-verifier<br/>publish gate"}}
    N --> V
    T --> TV{{"topic-validator<br/>redundancy"}}
    V --> P["npm run build → publish"]
    TV --> P
    B -. lessons .-> T
`}
/>

The loop closes on the dotted line: what a build teaches feeds back into the topic. That is the whole
point of having a workbench next to the garden.

## Content as data

The homepage grid and these logs are not hand-maintained lists. They read straight from the content
collections, so a new topic or build shows up with zero edits. The same dozen lines power every index:

```astro
---
// src/components/BuildLog.astro
import { getCollection } from 'astro:content';

const base = import.meta.env.BASE_URL.replace(/\/$/, ''); // "/working-intel"
const builds = (await getCollection('docs', (e) => e.id.startsWith('build/')))
  .sort((a, b) => (b.data.date?.getTime?.() ?? 0) - (a.data.date?.getTime?.() ?? 0));
---
{builds.map((b) => (
  <a href={`${base}/${b.id}/`}>{b.data.title}</a>
))}
```

## What broke

Three bugs worth keeping, because each is the kind that builds without a preview would have shipped:

- **`BASE_URL` has no trailing slash.** Building links as `` `${base}${id}` `` produced
  `/working-intelbuild/...` and a wall of 404s. The site is served under a base path, so every internal
  link has to join through a normalized separator. Easy to assume, expensive to miss.
- **zsh does not word-split unquoted variables.** A `for f in $files` loop over a multi-line file list
  silently ran once on the whole blob and edited nothing, while a sibling check reported success. Lesson:
  for bulk file edits, `find -exec` beats a shell loop.
- **Splash pages still render a page `<h1>`.** The homepage showed a giant "Working Intelligence" title
  on top of the terminal hero. The fix keeps the semantic heading for screen readers and SEO but hides it
  visually, leaving one visible title.

## The discipline that matters

The non-negotiable rule is **archive first, write second**. A note and a topic are interpreted artifacts,
which makes their claims hard to verify later. So the verbatim source is saved at ingestion, the writing
works from that archive, and an adversarial verifier checks every published claim against it before
anything ships. The archive stays private; only the sanitized synthesis goes public. Without that order,
you are grading your own homework.
